text:
In a small town surrounded by lush green hills, nestled at the foot of towering mountains, lived Martha. She made her home on a cozy porch overlooking a glittering lake. Martha was known for her beautiful butterfly gardens—their vibrant colors matched her cheerful disposition. Every garden bed had blooming flowers, but one species fascinated her more than all others: the mourning clovers.

One spring morning, as Martha watered her newest bloom festivals, she noticed something strange, something new. Small insects had gathered, and they weren’t your average butterflies—they were tiny brown things that fluttered heavily. Curiosity piqued, Martha approached.

They sang a melody. Nothing she’d heard before. They danced around her feet in an intricate pattern. Martha listened. They weren’t songs she recognized; they sounded like whispers.

Suddenly, one of them fluttered down to perch on her finger. Martha reached out and touched it. Its body buzzed. Martha smiled; she knew how bees liked honey.
